source.title: Which Actigraphy Dimensions Predict Longitudinal Outcomes in Bipolar Disorders?

confidence_rationale: Prospective longitudinal study (n=69, median 3.5 year follow-up) using principal component analysis to identify actigraphy dimensions that predict time to recurrence. Key finding: circadian variability/stability dimension (not total sleep time) significantly predicts recurrence (p=0.009), with intra-day variability (IV) as strongest individual predictor (AUC 0.75 alone, 0.82 combined with clinical variables). This directly supports Q-033's claim that sleep-architecture-proxying measures outperform simpler duration metrics for clinical decompensation prediction. Confidence moderate: the study only recorded 21 days of actigraphy (not long-run), sample is relatively small (n=69), and 'circadian variability' is a proxy for architecture not a direct staging measure. The study establishes predictive specificity for recurrence timing but does not address episode type discrimination.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In euthymic bipolar disorder patients (n=69), actigraphy-derived circadian variability/stability dimension predicts time to mood episode recurrence (p=0.009); intra-day variability alone achieves AUC=0.75, rising to AUC=0.82 combined with clinical variables (age, BD subtype, mood stabilizer polytherapy, mood episode density, BMI).
mapping.ree_translation: Q-033 proposes that a sleep-phase sufficiency mismatch index will predict decompensation timing with better specificity than total sleep time. This study shows that: (1) circadian rhythm dimension (not sleep duration) is the actigraphy predictor of recurrence; (2) the key metric is fragmentation/variability (IV) rather than amount; and (3) combining actigraphy with clinical variables adds meaningful discriminative power. This is structurally analogous to Q-033's mismatch index: sleep-phase signals combined with burden/context variables predict decompensation timing better than duration alone.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The actigraphy was recorded at a single 21-day baseline, not continuously over the follow-up period. Q-033 envisions long-run continuous monitoring with dynamic mismatch estimation -- a fundamentally different design. The 'intra-day variability' predictor is a circadian rhythm stability measure, related to but distinct from sleep-phase sufficiency. Episode type specificity was not the focus: all recurrences were pooled.
mapping.source_context: Euthymic bipolar disorder outpatients, French cohort, 78.3% BD-I, 59.4% female, median age 42 years, median follow-up 3.5 years (range 2-4.5 years), 63.8% experienced recurrence with median survival 17.8 months.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality good: prospective, long follow-up, Journal of Clinical Medicine peer-reviewed, statistical methods appropriate (PCA + survival analysis). Mapping fidelity good: directly demonstrates circadian dimension outperforms duration for decompensation timing prediction, which is the core structural claim in Q-033. Transfer risk moderate: baseline-only actigraphy vs Q-033's continuous model; small sample; homogeneous French BD cohort; pooled episode types vs Q-033's type-specific prediction.
